items.title: How Hershey's Ruined Reese's
items.description:
In February of 2026, the grandson of H.B. Reese, founder of Reese's Candy Company, publicly criticized Hershey’s, accusing the company of replacing core ingredients with cheaper substitutes; a claim Hershey's didn't even bother to deny. 

In this episode of Weird History, we dig into the growing controversy over alleged ingredient changes in popular chocolate bars. Are beloved sweets being quietly downgraded? From recipe shifts to consumer backlash, here’s what’s really going on behind the wrapp

items.title: A BREAKDOWN of ALL Symbols, Themes, and Commentary in No Other Choice
items.description:
In this No Other Choice Explained / 어쩔수가없다 Ending Explained and Analysis, I deep-dive analyze the Korean film, No Other Choice 2025, starring Lee Byung-hun and Son Ye-jin. Directed by Park Chan-wook, the film is a perfect blend of period drama, romance, and fantasy, exploring themes of humility, tradition, and corporate cruelty. I believe this film is excellent in capturing the themes of fatherhood and masculinity through great writing, acting, directing and filmmaking.

Furthermore, in this No

themes.name: The Past is a Costume Drama
themes.tagline: Historians, fashion critics, and literary detectives collide in the most stylish corner of YouTube.

items.title: Historian Guided Tour of Shakespeare's House
items.description:
Alice Loxton and Dan Snow head to Stratford-upon-Avon to uncover the secrets of William Shakespeare’s early life and upbringing. Who were Shakespeare’s parents? What was rural Warwickshire like in the 16th century, and how was it changing? What sort of childhood did William have?

To get to the bottom of these questions, Alice and Dan visit the gems of historic Warwickshire - a monumental medieval tithe barn, the village which Shakespeare’s father spent his childhood, and even the room where Wil

morning_soundtrack.title: Mallory Hawk – “Four O’Clocks”
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
Former Customer frontwoman Mallory Hawk's debut solo album Chinook is named after the military helicopters that dotted the skies around her childhood hometown. That town factors directly into "Four O'Clocks," the pensive and pretty new single she released today. Let Hawk explain:
The post Mallory Ha
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The sound of watching military helicopters disappear into a grey childhood sky, years later, from a safe distance.

morning_soundtrack.title: Skullcrusher Covers Tim Buckley For Sounds Of Saving
morning_soundtrack.snippet: By their own description, the nonprofit Sounds Of Saving "designs and provides tools, content, resources, and curriculum to young people, ages 14 to 24, on how to discover and utilize music to support their mental health and wellness." They have a music series called Song That Found Me At The Right
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: A fragile, candlelit cover that feels like a lifeline passed quietly between strangers.

morning_soundtrack.title: Velocity Girl Share Previously Unreleased “I Know Exactly What You Mean” From New 1989-1992 Comp
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Life comes at you with velocity, girl. How has it already been 30 years since the breakup of Washington, DC indie-pop greats Velocity Girl? The group, which briefly reunited in 2002, has been back in action lately. In 2023, they played their first show in 21 years, then played some more. In 2024, th
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Dusting off a mixtape you made at 19 and realizing it still hits exactly the same.

morning_soundtrack.title: In The Deep End With Rodney Crowell
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Rodney Crowell’s been around the block, literally and figuratively. As a sometimes secondary character in alternative country music’s schism from establishment norms in the 1970s, he formed fast friendships and collaborations with stalwarts like Guy Clark and Emmylou Harris (with whom
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Weathered and warm like a late-night conversation on a porch with someone who's lived enough to say something true.

morning_soundtrack.title: New David Bowie Collection to Feature Unreleased Songs with Jimmy Page
morning_soundtrack.snippet: The Shel Talmy Recordings gathers material the late singer, then going by Davie Jones, made with the titular producer in 1965
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The thrill of finding a crumpled polaroid in a vintage shop that turns out to be of someone legendary.

morning_soundtrack.title: Angelo Badalamenti’s Blue Velvet Score Gets 40th Anniversary Vinyl Reissue
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
Forty years after Blue Velvet, Angelo Badalamenti's iconic score is returning on vinyl alongside a 48-track deluxe digital edition packed with previously unreleased music.
Angelo Badalamenti’s Blue Velvet Score Gets 40th Anniversary Vinyl Reissue
Alex Krinsky
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The velvet hum of dread beneath a small-town surface, best experienced alone after midnight with the volume low.

global_silver_linings.title: Humpback Whale Watching Surges in Brazil Where Population Has Grown 27-fold Since Whaling Ended
global_silver_linings.snippet: The population of humpback whales around the world continues to climb ever higher, as do the records for numbers of sightings in single whale-watching seasons. In Brazil’s renowned Guanabara Bay, whale watching trips are becoming popular thanks to an increase in the population of humpbacks from 2,000 in 1986, to around 35,000 today. Every year, […] The post Humpback Whale Watching Surges in Brazil Where Population Has Grown 27-fold Since Whaling Ended appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: A 27-fold increase in humpback whales off Brazil's coast is a remarkable testament to what happens when we simply stop harming a species and let nature heal itself. The fact that communities can now build a thriving whale-watching culture where there was once industrial hunting is proof that recovery, even from near-extinction, is genuinely possible.

global_silver_linings.title: Gen Xer psychiatrist says we don’t actually miss the ’80s, we miss these 6 things
global_silver_linings.snippet: "1. The freedom to be unreachable." The post Gen Xer psychiatrist says we don’t actually miss the ’80s, we miss these 6 things appeared first on Upworthy .
global_silver_linings.reason: A psychiatrist's reminder that nostalgia is really a longing for specific feelings — connection, simplicity, autonomy — is quietly empowering, because those things aren't locked in the past. Understanding what we're actually craving gives us a real chance to cultivate it in our lives right now.

discovery_articles.title: Pont de la Bâtiaz in Martigny, Switzerland
discovery_articles.snippet: In the small city of Martigny is a wonderfully pleasant bridge named 'le Pont de la Bâtiaz'. Its outward beauty aside, it is the last wooden bridge in the entire French speaking canton of Valais that can still withstand vehicular usage. Spanning the Drance river, several different iterations of the bridge have existed at the location.

discovery_articles.title: Rex, the Beach Blvd Dinosaur in Jacksonville, Florida
discovery_articles.snippet: Rex the Dinosaur is regarded as one of Jacksonville’s most lovable roadside landmark. Built in the 1960s, he was originally installed as part of Goony Golf, a mini-golf and family-fun complex which closed its doors and putting greens in the late 1990s. When the land was purchased for redevelopment, locals protested his destruction and, in 2007, Rex was not only saved but restored with help from students in the University of North Florida's Building Construction Management program.

discovery_articles.title: Blackjack Cemetery    in Taft, Oklahoma
discovery_articles.snippet: Resting place of Sarah Rector, who was known as the richest Black girl in the world at one point. Resting place for Muscogee Creek Freedmen, and other Black children made wealthy by oil. In the middle of the historic Black town of Taft.

discovery_articles.title: ’Tropfsteinmaschine, 1996-2496‘ in Hamburg, Germany
discovery_articles.snippet: The Hamburger Kunsthalle houses almost 91,000 works of art. The most famous of these is undoubtedly Caspar David Friedrich’s 'Wanderer über dem Nebelmeer' (Wanderer Above the Sea of Fog). However, among the various collections on display, one modest piece might easily pass unnoticed in the Contemporary Art section.

discovery_articles.title: Ancient Egyptians Believed That These Newly Discovered Gold Tongues Allowed the Dead to Communicate in the Afterlife
discovery_articles.snippet: Excavations at Marina el-Alamein, an archaeological site west of Alexandria, unearthed about two dozen of the rare artifacts, including one shaped like the Eye of Horus

discovery_articles.title: In a First, Scientists Witness the Seafloor Spread in Real Time, Giving Them a Rare Glimpse at a Mysterious Geologic Process
discovery_articles.snippet: Across a matter of days in 2024, the seafloor in part of the Indian Ocean dropped by about 13 feet, and roughly 5.7 billion cubic feet of molten rock rose to the crust's surface, according to a new study

discovery_articles.title: Once the World’s Biggest Passenger Ship, This Metal Marvel That Crossed the Atlantic and Took Immigrants to Australia Has a New Museum Home
discovery_articles.snippet: A new museum dedicated to stories surrounding the S.S. "Great Britain" is about to open in Bristol, where the ship is permanently dry-docked

discovery_articles.title: Scientists Ranked Hundreds of Spider Species by Running Speed. Australia's Huge, Hairy Brown Huntsman Came Out on Top
discovery_articles.snippet: The brown huntsman can sprint at a maximum speed of eight miles per hour, although it can only maintain that pace for a fraction of a second. The research will lead to a better understanding of the evolution and biomechanics of arachnids

discovery_articles.title: The 2024 New York City meteorite contains amino acids
discovery_articles.snippet: The brine-formed meteorite that crashed into a New Jersey roof in 2024 could teach us about how life first arrived on Earth.

featured_read.title: How to Get Love Less Wrong: George Saunders on Breaking the Patterns that Break Our Hearts
featured_read.snippet: "We want to believe that love is singular and exclusive, and it unnerves us to think that it might actually be renewable..."
featured_read.blurb: George Saunders quietly dismantles the story we tell ourselves about love being a finite, once-given thing — and what it might mean to stop mistaking our patterns for our fate. Read it slowly, somewhere you won't be interrupted.

garden_note.note: High summer in Zürich has a particular intensity to it — the linden trees have finished their heady bloom, but the kitchen garden is now gloriously unruly, tumbling with warmth and green. Step out before the heat settles in and catch the garden at its most generous.
in_season: Zucchini (Zucchetti) — harvest daily before they become marrows overnight
French beans — pick tender pods now for the finest flavour
Echinacea and rudbeckia — bold summer perennials coming into their own
Deadhead roses and water deeply at the base to keep a second flush coming
garden_note.sky_tonight: A new moon means inky-dark skies — ideal for spotting the Milky Way arching over the Zürichberg if you head somewhere with low light pollution.

puzzle.label: Fern's Morning Riddle
puzzle.prompt:
I rise without waking, fall without sleep,
I paint the window gold before the house stirs.
I am loudest in silence, gone by your first cup.
What am I?
puzzle.hint: Think of what greets the room before anyone in it does.

previous_puzzle.label: The Evening Enigma
previous_puzzle.prompt:
Two of these claims are genuinely true. One is a well-dressed impostor. Which is the lie?

1. Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landings than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.
2. A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus.
3. Honey bees can recognize individual human faces using the same feature-mapping process that humans use.
previous_puzzle.answer: Number 3 is the lie: bees can learn to distinguish human faces in laboratory experiments, but they do so by treating the face as a complex pattern of parts — a process quite different from the holistic face-processing system humans use.

fern_data.greeting: While the coffee's still steaming and the birds are making their usual unreasonable demands outside the window, settle in — today's canopy has rabbit holes to tumble down, history wearing surprisingly good costumes, and slow-burn projects that will make you want to build something with your hands.
